sentences.text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]
My name is now Arthur Kern.
I was born in Vienna, Austria, October 19, 1928.
I grew up in a family where I had one brother by the name of Fritz, and my parents.
And I had aunts, uncles, cousins.
So for the first 10 years of my life, everything was very, very nice, as far as we were concerned.
We had-- we-- my father had a knitting factory.
We were semi-religious.
He, although he worked on Saturday, on Shabbos, we went to-- we went to synagogue-- not all the time, but occasionally.
We had a kosher home.
But it was a very close type of family.
Went to school for the first four years of my life.
Had private lessons in piano, had private lessons in Hebrew.
I played a lot of soccer at that time.
I was a fairly good student.
It was just a very normal, what I would-- what I would say is a middle-class type of life.
My brother was three years older.
He was, at the time of occupation-- which was in March of 1938, this was when the Germans entered Austria with the Anschluss-- I was 10 and he was-- he was 13.
OK, sure.
We lived in an apartment which had-- it was huge.
It was a huge apartment.
It had like six or seven bedrooms.
It had a room for a-- we had a maid, we had a room for her.
In fact, one of things I used to do, against my mother's wishes, I had a bicycle-- I think from my sixth or seventh birthday, and we had a long hall.
And I would open all the doors to connecting rooms, and take my bicycle and ride down the hall.
Come back to the rooms, and drive my mother bananas, sort of.
Yes.
I was not exactly an exemplary child, because it-- I was also sort of a mischievous side.

I mean, things I did that I recall-- you know, for instance the heating system that we had was a-- was a what they call Kachelofen, which was a tile type of a stove-- which eventually was replaced by a more modern stove that was supposed to heat the whole apartment.
Well, that particular stove had a-- was burning coal-- coke, rather-- and had a separate piping for the-- for the venting.
And I took the piping off one day when my parents weren't home, and the whole house filled up with smoke.
And to me and my brother, it was a big lark.
My mother and my father came home and they were ready to kill me.
Other things I did as I recall were childish pranks.
I remember at that time, they had the horses and carts, along with street cars and automobiles-- they were already in existence.
But I remember that there were a lot of horses and carts in Vienna, and I used to-- I used to pet the horses.
Well, one day, I got the bright idea to take some of the-- to pet the horses, and take some hair from the tail of the horses.
I took them home, and my mother had bought like a dozen eggs, and I made a pinhole each one of the eggs and stuck a horse hair at the end of the-- at the-- into each one of the eggs.
Of course, when my mother started opening up the eggs, and she sees the hair in there, and she says, what is this?
It didn't take-- it didn't take too long for her to figure out who was behind it.
Of course, I had the tendency to blame my brother.
Were you punished?
My mother punished me pretty good.
My mother always-- she believed in spanking, and she was a pretty strict disciplinarian.
My father, on the other hand, was a very jovial person-- a very-- very outgoing.
All the kids loved him-- not just myself, my brother-- but all of my cousins.
And he was just an outgoing-- he was always a joker.
He always had candy in his pockets.
He was just a very a-- a very jovial type of a person.
He was very easy to get along with.
No, I lived-- in Vienna, they had different districts.
I lived in what they call the Ninth District, the 9th Bezirk.
It was not primarily a Jewish neighborhood.
We-- in fact, I think in our house, I doubt that there were maybe more than two or three Jewish families, if that many.
And in fact, I didn't know anyone in my house who was a Jewish family.
There may have been, I just don't recall.
But it was not particularly Jewish.
There was a Jewish neighborhood, a section, called the Second District, where most of the Jews lived.
My grandparents lived there, which is interesting because I-- even though I was only three years old when they died, I still remember not only them, but their apartment.
There are not too many things I remember that far back, but I do specifically member their apartment and my grandparents.
Yes.
Yes, my grandparents were really not very warm people.
They-- they would-- you know, we'd come to the house and they would--
My mother's parents.
My father's parents lived in Poland someplace.
And I only saw my father's mother one time when she came on a visit to Vienna.
But my mother's parents did live Vienna.
And like I said, they were on the cold side.
I could never remember them hugging or kissing me.
Or-- I also remember my grandfather-- once we were visiting-- my-- my parents, myself, and my brother were visiting my grandparents' house.
And my grandfather slapped my mother-- for what, I don't remember the details.
But they were just not very warm people.
I remember what they looked like.
I remember their apartment.
But I also remember there was nothing really very warm about them.
So that's my mother's parents.
My father's parents, I never met my grandfather on my father's side.
Like I said, my grandmother on my father's side came to Austria one time, to Vienna.
She was supposed to stay with my father's brother who also lived in Vienna.
And they had a son the same age as I was, 10 years-- well, at that time, he was only about eight years, and we were both eight years old.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
My grandmother did not want to stay at his house very long, because my cousin was even worse behaved then I was.
So they moved over to our apartment.
And I remember my father and mother telling me that I had better be on my best behavior, otherwise they were going to kill me.
So for about a week, I was able to stand and behave fairly well.
But that's only time I met my maternal grandmother.
So she came to stay in your apartment?
Yes, she came to stay in our place.
And like I said, I had to behave for about a week.
But that is the only time I met my maternal-- my paternal grandmother.
I really don't-- it's very vague, because I only knew her for about approximately one week.
I do know that she had cancer, that she died from cancer.
Also, I found out recently, that my father, in addition to the one brother he had in Vienna, there were four other brothers in Poland, either in-- probably in Lvov-- which is Lemberg.
And that one of them was a professor of mathematics at one of the universities, but I don't know where.
Now, I knew nothing really about these four brothers, until fairly recently, when the one surviving cousin who is the same age as I am, has told me about it.
Because he and his family did get out of Austria in 1939.
But I'll get into that later on.
No, but you know, there's really no occasion to mention them.
You don't normally talk about things like that to your kids.
You know-- we were a close family, but there was one of my father's-- one brother of my father's was living in Vienna.
I had, on my mother's side, I had several aunts and uncles.
So those people who were in Vienna, we were always in communication with them.
We talked about different things, but it was just not mentioned.
One of my uncles was quite a bit older-- on my mother's side-- was quite a bit older than the rest of them.
So my cousins had their cousins which are like 15, 20 years older than I am, and I have very little recollection of any of them.
It's primarily-- I was the youngest one of the-- of the children amongst the cousins.
So--
Yeah.
There was-- I never felt any kind of anti-Semitism when I was growing up, before the Anschluss.
I'm sure it existed, because I'd heard about it.
But I went to school.
I was-- like the good students, would sit near the front of the class.
I was near the front of the class.
I was good in gym.
I had good grades.
I had friends.
We used to play marbles, we used to play football-- well, soccer at the time.
And well, my brother, myself, the other kids, they were mostly non-Jewish.
No particular problem with any of that.
Not until after the Anschluss.
So as far as my life is concerned, I really had a very, very nice childhood, until I was 10 years old.
I had things that other people didn't have.
Of course, in retrospect, I recognize now that I was of the fortunate ones, because there were a lot of poor people in Austria, also, and a lot of poor Jewish people in Austria.
And we were, like I said, middle class.
We never lacked food.
We never lacked-- I never-- I didn't lack toys, I didn't lack family.
I had really a very exemplary life.
It was delightful.
Yeah we went-- no, not the rabbi, but I remember the synagogue.
It was a-- it was a large synagogue, and it was in the 20th District.
And we used to go there, occasionally.
It was a beautiful temple.
I don't think it exists anymore today.
Detailed recollections, I really don't have of that.
Well, right after the Anschluss-- after the Anschluss, in March-- when Hitler took over Austria, in March of 1938, things almost immediately started changing.

Just prior to the Anschluss, for instance, there were-- there were Austrian patriots who had painted all over the sidewalks the saying, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, 'Red, white, red, until we die'-- which is the Austrian flag-- red, white, red.

Right after the Anschluss, the Germans started picking up Jewish women, and had them-- gave them pails and brushes, and had them on their knees, scrub the-- scrub the sidewalks to get rid of those slogans.
I saw a lot of that going on, and it started being very spooky.
Almost immediately, the Jewish children, for instance-- amongst other things, they had religion was one the classes that they had in public school.
Well, the Jewish kids had their own religion classes.
That was abandoned immediately.
And in addition to that, the Jewish kids, no matter how good a student they were, all the Jewish kids now had to sit at the back of the class.
Suddenly, the kids I used to play soccer with, they didn't want to play with the Jewish kids anymore.
It just was a complete change.
You go into the-- within weeks, within a coupe of weeks or a couple months, you saw signs appearing in the-- in the parks, on the park benches, 'Juden verboten'-- no Jews allowed.
In stores, no entry to Jews.
And you know, no matter where you went, you saw sings of that nature.
I remember I finished the fourth grade in September of '38-- and after that the fifth grade, you start what they call like a high school.
And I'd taken the test, I had gotten very excellent grades, but neither my cousin-- who was the same as I was-- or myself were accepted.
They just wouldn't let Jews into the gymnasium, into these high schools.
So they said we all had to go to a special Jewish schools.
Well, Hitler Youth started-- the Hitler Youth gang started appearing.
And when you went to school, or when you left school, they would gather outside near the schools, and they would beat the hell out of us.
You know, we'd- some of us would fight back.
But on the other hand, I know kids whose parents told them, don't fight back.
Because if you do fight back, the parents can get arrested.
So I mean, there were all kinds of things going on at the time.
You know, you think that when you're 10 years old, you really don't know much about what's going on.
You don't necessarily recognize the extent, like an adult would.
But believe me, I knew what was going on.
I knew the dangers that had occurred.
One of my-- in the Kristallnacht, for instance, my father he'd-- my father had come home that night.
He and my uncle-- of my father-- he's his brother, my paternal uncle-- hid in their own factory for that particular night.
Now we, ourselves, nobody entered our apartment.
But there were a lot of people whose apartments were just trashed-- they would just tear everything-- tear sheets, overturn beds.
They would-- they would break all the dishes.
I mean, they just-- I mean, they just would leave a mess of the apartments.
They would beat up the people.
And so that night, one of my uncles was picked up and sent to Buchenwald.
And he was there for-- for a while.
At that time, if you could get-- if you could get exit visas, they were still release you from concentration camps.
And this particular uncle, his wife, they had sent their daughter to England-- their daughter was, at the time, 15 years old, and they sent her with the children's transport.
And the daughter came-- came to family, who helped her parents get a visa to England.
So my uncle, at the time, was released from the concentration camps.
And I still have a copy of the release certificate from-- from Buchenwald, which I keep with my memoirs.
Anyway, after they were released, I remember them coming over to our house and telling my parents, just leave.
Leave everything you've got here-- just leave.
You just don't know how bad it can be.
And they were also told, if they would talk about their experiences in Buchenwald, they would be immediately arrested.
So they didn't even want to talk about it.
They just wanted to say, just leave.
Leave everything.

Now, that time also, right after Kristallnacht-- possibly before-- the Germans, what they would do is, if Jews had a factory or store, they would put in one of their own cronies to start running it and take it over.

So basically, my father's factory, a man was installed called a [GERMAN], who basically was now the boss of the factory, and my father to report to him, and you know, he would take care of-- this German guy would take care of all the finances-- start decreasing the amount of money my father would get, and so forth.
So he no longer had control of the factory, either.
My father at the time, after my uncle had told him to leave-- just get, out because you don't know how bad it can get-- they decided-- well, at first they tried to get out.
They tried to get to England and the United States, but the visas just didn't come.
Now he had, I remember, he could have gotten into Paraguay, could have gotten into Uruguay-- but they thought it's going to blow over.
So it wasn't-- plus the fact that he was making still a living over there.
It's very difficult to think at that age, to just leave everything and start over in a different country-- with a couple of kids, not speaking the language.
And you don't know what the future will bring.
And you take the choices you have to make, which is going to be worse for you, which is going to be better.
Oh, I'm sure they thought that they would have blown over, because if they would have known what would happen, I'm sure they would have left.
Slowly but surely, other things happened.
Like, I had one of my mother's cousins, the Kosners, who were kicked out of their apartment.
They had no place to go.
So they came to live with us.
In February of 1939-- January or February of 1939-- I think my parents had decided, that's it.
I think we better get out.
By that time, they tried to get visas to Shanghai-- Uruguay would no longer take them.
Paraguay would no longer take them.
The United States, they were still trying to get into.
They couldn't get visas.
In addition, you could possibly get visas, if you had the money.
But what happened with my parents, since this German guy took over the factory, I mean, they confiscated everything they had.
So there was just no-- no place to go.
Well, this is a time when a lot of the parents tried to get at least the kids out.
And there were a lot of children who went to England on children's transports-- to Belgium, the Netherlands.
And my parents tried to get me onto these transports-- my brother and myself.

And through the the Austrian, the [GERMAN] the Jewish [GERMAN], found out about transports that France was taking some kids, and tried to get my brother and myself onto this-- onto a children's transport to France.
I have the paper-- I have the application still that shows-- I have the application from-- from 1939, the application to-- for me to go to France.
My-- now, my brother at the time was 13, I was 10 and a half.
The organization would only take children up to the age of 13.
Once you're 13, they deemed you're just too old.
Excuse me.
So consequently, my brother didn't get out.
But I got on a transport into France.
I want you to tell us a little bit, if you would, about what you remember actually leaving, and what your parents told you, and your response to leaving.
I don't want you to rush through that.
No, OK.
Well, several things.

Number one, my parents-- the idea was that they were going to send me out and my brother out-- of course, he didn't go-- but they were going to get visas, follow me, pick me up in France, and eventually would go to the United States-- someplace.
I also remember, well, actually, the night before I left, I did several things.
One of them, I was very happy for.
One of them is still on my conscience as-- it followed me around for a long, long time.
The good thing I did, is I surreptitiously went through my parents' picture album and took out photos of different people who were dear to me-- my grandparents, my parents, and my aunts and uncles.
It is the only thing that I really have left of my parents.
Most of the other children who came to France didn't do that.
And most of them don't have any kind of records or any kind of pictures.
But I carried those pictures with me throughout my-- until I came to the United States.
And of course, I have them today.
The other thing which I did, that I remember very well, are writing a sort of a farewell letter, and leaving it in my-- in my room.
And I specifically remember things like, where I said, today I'm 10 years old, and I must leave my parents' house.
I found out later on that when my mother had read the letter, she actually--she absolutely came apart.
I mean, she really, it really affected her.
To this day, it may have been a childish thing to do, but I recognized even at that time that we have to get out, that, otherwise, some bad things are going to happen.
And I wrote the letter saying, at this young age, I had to leave my parents' house.
And it's-- that letter-- even though I don't have a copy of the letter, obviously, but the thought has just followed me around, especially after I found out the reaction of my mother.
Yes, March 14, 1939.
I was in the transport-- I was put on the transport in the train in Vienna.
My parents and my brother came with me to train.
Of course, I, you know, we all stuck our heads out of the window.
There were 50 of us on the transport, on this children's transport.
We all stuck our heads out of the window.
And of course, the parents were waiving goodbye as the train left.
And the last thing I remember is my father sort of jogging after the train on the platform.
And he always wore a pen and pencil set in his suit.
And his-- his pen and pencil set fell out of his jacket pocket.
He bent down to pick it up.
And then the train curved-- it curved around the corner.
And that was the last time I saw them.
No, no, no.
The idea was that they were going to follow me.
I had no reason to-- I mean, I had been away from my parents before.
For instance, in '36 or '37, they'd sent me and my brother to Italy for the whole summer on a vacation, to a children's camp.
And it was a very pleasant experience.
Now, I recognize, this was very different-- but I was very sure that they were going to follow me at some point in time.
Maybe three months, four months, they'll pick me up and we'll start life someplace else.

Of course, I did not recognize at the time that, even if they had come and picked me up and started a life someplace else, my life would have been very, very different than it had been as a middle class youngster growing up in Vienna.
But I knew my way around Vienna, for instance, very well.
I took street cars, and I used to go all over the place.
I mean, at 10 years old, I knew Vienna like inside out.
It was a perfectly safe city at the time to run around in, so we had, I had a great time.
Well, the train trip itself was not-- was not very traumatic, really.
I mean, there were 50 of us.
There were some kids-- there were children as young as five or six years old.
And the oldest was 12.
Since then, I've read many articles and books about the situation.

The children who-- they picked children of certain ages, because they felt they were the most vulnerable-- well, they were the most vulnerable, and still the French organizations were going to be able to take care of them.
For instance, they wouldn't take babies-- so people who couldn't-- kids who couldn't feed themselves, people who had sicknesses, and so forth.
They also felt that-- that children over 13 were big enough that they could manage better on their own, if they had to, than children under that.
So that's why, I guess, they picked these particular age group to send them on the children's transport.
Now, the English, I think they took children up to the age of about 15 or 16.
But the two transports that left Austria from Vienna, they only took them up to about the age of 12-- up to 12, up to and including the age of 12.
I remember we arrived and-- and some kids were crying, you know.
But I was not particularly unhappy.
I was leaving Austria, I was leaving a situation which was-- which was already getting pretty bad.
You know-- I don't like to get beaten up in school.
I couldn't go outside and play anymore, because you'd always be afraid that the Hitler Youth gangs would come along.
So going to France really was a very pleasant thing for me.
We arrived in France through Strasbourg.
And I remember the border crossing, and kids making comments-- my god, we could've taken all kinds of things with us, you know, jewelry, and money and whatever, because we weren't really searched.
What was this date?
We arrived in-- we arrived in Paris, at the train station, and were taken to a-- by buses-- to a-- well, no.
We arrived-- yeah, we arrived in Paris, and were taken to-- first to a Rothschild hospital for approximately three or four days.
Where, amongst other things there, they tried to give us-- tried to give us exams, physical exams.
And they really tried to clean us out by giving us all laxatives.
And I remember the first night with-- with all of us trying to use the one or two bathrooms, it was a mess.

But after that, what they did is, after approximately three or four days, we were sent to an old cloister, and I believe it was run by French organization called called, Oeuvre de Secours aux Enfants.
Which means, it's an organization which helps children.
The abbreviation is OSE-- and the German pronunciation is Oh-zay.
So amongst our whole group, everybody calls it the Oh-zay.
So when I say Oh-zay, that's what it means, is this particular French organization.
We were sent to a place which was an old cloister, and it was at the outskirts of Paris, and it was called, Chateau Maubuisson.
We were there approximately one month.
But while we were there, other children started coming in, primarily children from Berlin, from a Berlin transport.
And I remember a transport from Frankfurt that came in.
And, you know, kids are funny.
The Austrians stayed by themselves, the Germans stayed by themselves.
But after a while, things started mixing up a little bit, because you started looking for-- in my particular case, and also the other kids-- you were looking for friends.
And you wanted kids your own age.
You wanted-- like if I was a 10-year-old boy, I was looking for 10-year-old boys.
A seven-year-old girl would look for seven-year-old girls.
The older kids didn't want to talk to the younger kids or play with them.
I mean, so it was a-- so things got pretty mixed up, after a while.
So we started making friends.
All the kids were in the same boat-- they all had-- their parents had sent them away.

It-- I think, in my estimation today, I think it must've been very, very difficult for the parents to send the children away at ages, you know, six, seven, eight years old, or 12 old-- whatever they were.
Because the first thing the parent had to do was to recognize that he's no longer in the best position to take care of his own child.
And that must be a very, very difficult thing to do.
So I think it was very difficult on the parents, also.

I mean, I think it was more difficult on the parents than on the children, because at least as children, we had-- when we came together and started making friends, and we were now in France, and we had a very different life.
And it--- we no longer had the pressures of this anti-Semitism that we experienced, that I experienced in Austria.
When we were in Chateau Maubuisson, the transport that had come from Vienna, about 50 kids, they divided us into two segments.
I do not know why, I do not know how we were chosen, but there were two different segments.
One group went to the OSE.
And I was in that group that went to the OSE in the outskirts of Paris-- in a-- it was a-- a home, basically a children's home.
It was called Villa Helvetia.
And it was in a town on the outside, outskirts of Paris called-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] now I can't remember the name of the town.
